personality_voice.book_baseline: Book Harry is brave, curious, and loyal, but also deeply self-conscious about his fame, prone to guilt, capable of pettiness (his resentment of Cedric over Cho), and possessed of a dry, understated humor. He is reactive rather than passive: he pushes back against authority when he feels wronged, snaps at Ron during their estrangement, and internally narrates with irony and self-awareness. His voice is direct and short-sentenced under stress, and he frequently suppresses emotion until it bursts. He is also notably stubborn — refusing Bagman's help, insisting on telling Cedric about the dragons, refusing to bow to Voldemort. His internal monologue reveals insecurity, longing for a normal life, and a recurring awareness of his own mortality.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves Harry's core bravery, loyalty, and directness. His refusal to bow to Voldemort ('No'), his insistence on bringing Cedric's body back, his anger at Ron ('You're being stupid'), and his dry humor ('I love magic') are all present. His stubbornness is shown when he refuses Bagman's help and when he tells Cedric about the dragons. The film Harry is recognizably the same person.
personality_voice.difference: The film strips away most of Harry's internal voice. His self-consciousness about fame, his guilt over Sirius, his petty jealousy of Cedric over Cho, his longing for a parent figure, and his dry internal commentary are all absent because the medium removes narration. His emotional complexity — the shame about wanting Sirius to come back, the deliberate suppression of worry so as not to alarm Ron and Hermione — is not conveyed. His voice in dialogue is slightly more declarative and less hesitant than in the books. The film also softens his pettiness: the book Harry is genuinely unpleasant to Ron during the estrangement in ways the film compresses.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality traits are preserved and observable in dialogue and action. The loss is primarily of texture and internal contradiction rather than of defining traits. The film Harry is recognizable and consistent. A moderate penalty applies for the flattening of his internal contradictions and the loss of his self-conscious, ironic inner voice, but the main traits — bravery, stubbornness, loyalty, directness — are intact.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Harry is the primary agent of the story. He investigates Crouch's disappearance, decodes the egg clue himself (with help), tells Cedric about the dragons, refuses Bagman's assistance, writes to Sirius, visits Dumbledore's Pensieve, and makes the key moral decision to share the cup with Cedric. He drives scenes rather than being driven through them. He also initiates the confrontation with Voldemort by refusing to play along and choosing to fight rather than hide.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Harry retains his central role. He tells Cedric about the dragons, refuses Bagman's help, chooses to share the cup, brings Cedric's body back, and reports to Dumbledore. His agency in the graveyard — refusing to bow, fighting back, breaking the Priori Incantatem connection — is preserved.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Several significant agency transfers occur. In the books, Harry works out the egg clue himself through sustained effort (the bath scene, Myrtle's hint, his own deduction). In the film, Cedric gives him the bath hint and Neville provides the gillyweed with explicit explanation, making Harry more passive in the problem-solving. The Pensieve scene is compressed: in the books Harry falls in accidentally and witnesses three full trials, giving him crucial information he then acts on; in the film it is shorter and less consequential to his agency. The investigation of Crouch's disappearance — where book Harry actively pieces together clues and writes to Sirius — is largely absent from the film. Moody's role in guiding Harry through the maze is present in both, but the film makes it more explicit.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Harry remains the protagonist and primary agent in the film's key scenes. However, the transfer of problem-solving agency (egg clue, gillyweed) to Cedric and Neville is a meaningful reduction. The Transfer Rule applies: Cedric's explicit bath advice and Neville's explicit gillyweed explanation replace Harry's own sustained effort. This is a moderate penalty, not severe, because Harry still acts on the information and the key moral and physical choices remain his.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Harry's motivations in GoF are layered: he wants to survive the tournament he didn't enter; he wants to protect Sirius from being caught; he is driven by guilt (over Cedric, over Sirius's return); he is motivated by a desire to be believed and to prove he didn't cheat; he is motivated by loyalty to friends; and he is driven by a deep, rarely articulated longing for a normal life and for parental figures. His internal conflict includes the tension between wanting glory (his fantasies about winning) and knowing he didn't earn his place, and the tension between self-preservation and moral obligation (rescuing all hostages, sharing the cup).
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves Harry's desire to survive, his loyalty to friends, and his moral obligation (rescuing Gabrielle, sharing the cup). His anger at not being believed is present. His concern for Sirius is shown in the fireplace scene.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film largely removes Harry's internal conflict about wanting glory while knowing he didn't earn it — his fantasies about winning the tournament are absent. His guilt about Sirius returning is compressed into a single scene. His longing for a parent figure (one of the most poignant book motivations, culminating in his reaction to the echo of his parents in the graveyard) is present in the graveyard but not built up across the year. The film does not show his deliberate suppression of worry to protect Ron and Hermione, which is a key motivational texture in the books.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The core motivations — survival, loyalty, moral obligation — are intact and drive the same actions. The internal conflict about glory versus legitimacy is absent, which is a meaningful simplification. The motivations are simplified but not wrong. A moderate penalty applies.

character_arc.book_baseline: Harry's arc in GoF moves from a boy who is famous but relatively sheltered from the true weight of Voldemort's return, through a year of isolation, fear, and moral testing, to a boy who has looked Voldemort in the face, lost someone, and emerged traumatized but not broken. Key arc steps: the scar pain and dream (foreshadowing), the tournament as a trap, the estrangement from Ron (testing his resilience), the graveyard (the central trauma), and the aftermath (grief, Dumbledore's speech, the decision to go on). The arc ends with Harry fundamentally changed — he has witnessed death, survived Voldemort, and is now carrying a burden the wizarding world is not yet ready to share.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film follows the same arc trajectory. Harry begins relatively carefree, is thrust into danger, survives the graveyard, witnesses Cedric's death, and ends the film changed and sobered. Dumbledore's speech and Harry's final exchange with Hermione ('Everything's going to change now, isn't it?') mark the arc's conclusion.
character_arc.difference: The arc is compressed. The estrangement from Ron is shortened and its emotional weight reduced. The sustained buildup of dread across the year — Harry's growing anxiety, his isolation, his repeated scar pain — is compressed into a few scenes. The aftermath of the graveyard is handled quickly: the hospital wing scene, the Diggorys' visit, and Harry's grief are all present but abbreviated. The arc's emotional depth is present but rushed, particularly the transition from trauma to the decision to go on.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc exists and is faithful in its shape and endpoint. The compression loses some important steps — particularly the sustained buildup of dread and the extended aftermath — but the arc is not altered or falsified. A moderate penalty for compression that removes important emotional steps.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Harry's key relationships in GoF: (1) Ron — best friendship tested by jealousy and estrangement, resolved through shared danger; (2) Hermione — steady, intellectual friendship, she is his most reliable support; (3) Sirius — godfather as surrogate parent, Harry's emotional anchor; (4) Dumbledore — mentor, source of truth and protection; (5) Cedric — rival turned ally, whose death is the emotional climax; (6) Voldemort — the defining antagonist relationship, now direct and physical.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Ron estrangement and reconciliation are present. Hermione's steady support is shown. The Sirius fireplace scene preserves their bond. Dumbledore's mentorship is present. The Cedric relationship — from rivals to allies sharing the cup — is faithfully rendered. The Voldemort confrontation is present.
key_relationships.difference: The Sirius relationship is significantly compressed. In the books, Harry writes multiple letters, receives multiple replies, and the relationship is a sustained emotional thread across the year — Sirius is Harry's primary confidant and the source of crucial information. In the film, there is one fireplace scene. The emotional weight of Harry's longing for Sirius as a parent figure is present in the scene but not built across the year. The Dumbledore relationship loses the Pensieve scene's depth — in the books, Harry and Dumbledore have a long, intimate conversation after the Pensieve that deepens their bond; in the film it is shorter. The Ron estrangement is present but compressed, losing some of the specific texture of Harry's hurt and stubbornness.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationships are all present and their essential dynamics are preserved. The Sirius relationship is the most significantly compressed, losing its sustained emotional thread. The Dumbledore relationship loses some depth. These are meaningful compressions but not distortions of the dynamics.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The books give Harry considerable complexity in GoF: his petty jealousy of Cedric over Cho; his deliberate manipulation of Uncle Vernon using Sirius's name; his self-aware embarrassment about wanting a parent figure; his internal fantasy about winning the tournament while knowing he didn't earn it; his sustained guilt about Sirius; his dry, self-deprecating humor; his stubborn refusal to ask for help even when he needs it; his capacity for genuine cruelty to Ron during the estrangement; his terror in the graveyard rendered in granular physical and psychological detail; his grief after Cedric's death rendered over multiple days.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves Harry's bravery, loyalty, stubbornness, and moral fiber. His humor is present in small moments. His grief after Cedric is shown. His refusal to bow to Voldemort is a strong character moment.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film loses: the Dursley manipulation scene (showing Harry's cunning and his complicated relationship with his own powerlessness); the sustained internal fantasy about winning; the petty jealousy of Cedric; the extended grief aftermath; the granular psychological texture of the graveyard (Harry's internal resistance to Voldemort's Imperius, his deliberate choice to die standing rather than crouching); the Pensieve scene's full complexity (three trials, Harry's growing understanding of the wizarding world's moral failures); the SPEW subplot's effect on Harry (showing his pragmatic, slightly cynical side); and the sustained buildup of dread across the year. These are not trivial losses — they constitute much of what makes book Harry a rich, contradictory character rather than a straightforwardly heroic one.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The film Harry is recognizable and faithful in his core traits, but the removal of his internal contradictions, his pettiness, his cunning, and his sustained psychological texture constitutes a meaningful flattening. He becomes more straightforwardly heroic and less interestingly flawed. This is a noticeable simplification that affects the audience's understanding of who he is, though it does not fundamentally misrepresent him.

lost_or_transferred_material: Harry's sustained effort to decode the egg clue (transferred to Cedric's explicit bath advice)
Harry's acquisition of gillyweed through his own research (transferred to Neville's explicit explanation)
Harry's manipulation of Uncle Vernon using Sirius's name (entirely absent)
Harry's internal fantasy about winning the Triwizard Tournament
Harry's petty jealousy of Cedric over Cho Chang
Harry's sustained guilt about Sirius returning to danger on his behalf
Harry's longing for a parent figure (present in graveyard but not built across the year)
Harry's deliberate internal resistance to Voldemort's Imperius Curse in the graveyard (the 'I won't' moment is present but its psychological texture is compressed)
Harry's extended grief aftermath over multiple days following Cedric's death
Harry's investigation of Barty Crouch's disappearance and active piecing together of clues
The full Pensieve sequence (three trials, Harry's growing understanding of the wizarding world's moral failures under Crouch)
Harry's dry, self-deprecating internal commentary throughout the year
Harry's capacity for genuine cruelty to Ron during the estrangement
The Dursley scenes establishing Harry's home life and his cunning use of Sirius as leverage
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function or act of agency is transferred to another character) considered: egg clue and gillyweed problem-solving transferred to Cedric and Neville respectively. Score kept below 80 in aggregate to reflect this.

Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove a major part of complexity, agency, or nuance) applied: the removal of Harry's internal contradictions, sustained psychological texture, and key agency moments prevents a score above 85.
key_observations: The film's Harry Potter is a faithful but simplified version of the book character. His core traits — bravery, loyalty, stubbornness, moral fiber, directness — are preserved and consistently demonstrated. The key dramatic moments (refusing to bow to Voldemort, sharing the cup with Cedric, bringing Cedric's body back, the graveyard confrontation) are all present and handled faithfully. The primary losses are: (1) the transfer of problem-solving agency to Cedric and Neville, making Harry more passive in the tournament's intellectual challenges; (2) the removal of his internal contradictions and self-aware complexity, particularly his pettiness, his guilt, and his longing for a parent figure as a sustained thread; (3) the compression of his key relationships, especially with Sirius; and (4) the loss of the granular psychological texture that makes book Harry a genuinely flawed and interesting protagonist rather than a straightforwardly heroic one. The film is recognizably faithful but has flattened the character into his most heroic and least contradictory aspects.
